moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Grob Opening, an offbeat choice that immediately challenges Black's center while preparing to fianchetto our bishop on g2. This move is unconventional—we're not contesting the center directly with e4 or d4, but instead we're staking a claim on the kingside and setting up a long-range influence along the a1-h8 diagonal. The key idea is that our g2 bishop will aim at the long diagonal while we maintain flexibility in the center, though we do accept that we're surrendering space and must play actively to justify this unusual approach.

Black immediately strikes at the center with a strong pawn push, which is the most natural response to our fianchetto setup. By playing d5, Black claims space and prevents us from easily establishing a pawn on e4, while also preparing to develop pieces with purpose. This move is the foundation of the Keene Defense—it's assertive and gives Black comfortable central control right away.

We continue our fianchetto plan by preventing Black from playing ...Bg4 (pinning our pieces or controlling key squares), and we also prepare to potentially push g5 later if opportunities arise. The move h3 is a prophylactic move that softens Black's setup: we're not interested in trading pieces immediately, but rather in preventing Black from dictating the tempo with threats. This also leaves open the option of g5 to gain space on the kingside if Black's pieces aren't well-coordinated.

Black extends the pawn center and gains space in the middle, preventing any future e4 idea from us and securing more territory. This central advance is logical—Black wants to establish a strong presence and make room for piece development, particularly the light-squared bishop. At this point, Black has a comfortable, space-advantaged position, so we'll need to find active piece play to compensate for our unusual opening choice.

We complete our fianchetto, placing the bishop on the long diagonal where it exerts pressure along a1-h8 and eyeballs Black's center, particularly the d5 pawn. This bishop is the engine of our opening strategy—it gives us active piece play despite our slow beginning, and it supports potential future advances like g5 or f4. Now our main plan is to develop pieces quickly (Nf3, c4 or a4) and create dynamic counterplay rather than passively accepting Black's space advantage.

Black plays a solid, prophylactic move that prepares ...Qxd4 ideas later and supports the d5 pawn while keeping options open for a future ...b5 push. This move is characteristic of the Keene Defense—Black isn't rushing to develop but instead consolidating the center and preparing a flexible middlegame plan. We're now facing a well-organized Black position, and we must continue developing actively (Nf3, d3, or c4) to create concrete threats and justify our unconventional opening.
